title: Tall-Back Wooden Planter Box
summary: A cedar picket planter with a dramatically taller back panel than front — built from two mirror-image leg frames, so the height difference is designed in, not trimmed in after.
description:
This planter's whole visual interest is the tall back panel leaning away from a much shorter front — the kind of asymmetry that reads as a display piece rather than a plain box. The trick is that the height difference lives entirely in the two leg frames: cut one pair of legs long in front and one pair long in back, build both frames identically otherwise, and the tall-back shape falls out of the frame geometry rather than needing an angled cut anywhere.

The upper support boards are the one detail worth double-checking before you fasten anything: they mount on the OUTSIDE of the frame, and — this is the part that's easy to miss — they're placed differently on each side, mirrored rather than identical. Dry-fit both frames side by side and confirm they mirror each other before driving a single screw.

Cedar fence pickets are the assumed stock throughout, sized to a nominal 5-1/2" width. Real pickets vary, so if yours run narrower, don't try to force the plan's exact spacing — space the gaps out evenly across whatever width you actually have, and the finished look holds up fine. The back boards are explicitly a cut-to-fit step, measured off the assembled frame rather than a fixed number, which is standard practice anywhere a plan's frame dimensions could drift slightly from the page.

tools.note: The back boards are measured and cut to fit the assembled frame.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Long cuts first — the back legs are the longest pieces.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" wide
materials.note: Per one planter. If your pickets measure narrower than 5-1/2", space the gaps out evenly instead of forcing this spacing.

cutList.note: The tall-back shape lives entirely in this length difference from the front legs.
Cut to fit the assembled frame rather than to this exact length.

steps.title: Build the two leg frames
steps.body: Build two identical leg-and-support frames — one with the short front legs, one with the tall back legs — using 2-1/2" self-tapping screws, two per joint.

steps.title: Add the upper supports
steps.body: Attach the upper support pieces to the outside of each frame. These mirror each other rather than mount identically — dry-fit both frames side by side and confirm the mirroring before you commit to screws.

steps.title: Skin the sides
steps.body:
Attach the side cedar pickets to the outsides of the two frames with 1-1/4" exterior fasteners and exterior wood glue.

This plan expects 5-1/2"-wide pickets. If yours run narrower, evenly space the gaps across the actual width instead of forcing the plan's spacing.

steps.title: Attach the front
steps.body: Attach the front cedar pickets to the fronts of both frames, keeping the outside edges flush.

steps.title: Cut and attach the back
steps.body:
Measure your assembled frame and cut the back pickets to fit — the tall-back panel is a cut-to-fit step, not a fixed length. Attach with 1-1/4" fasteners and exterior wood glue.

Sand and finish before planting.

images.alt: Wooden planter box with a tall back panel and shorter front
